title: I started a Crypto Club at school. Getting permission was the hard part.
bodyHtml:
The first time money caught my attention in a meaningful way was when I was in a vegetable market in Cuttack. A street vendor that had been selling at a corner for years was standing there that day just squinting at a QR code on his phone, trying to figure out why a payment had not gone through. He was confused and maybe a bit frustrated because of the fact something he had never asked for had quietly become the only option he had left.

A few years before this, none of this was even imaginable to most people. Cash was the only option for such informal markets. Then UPI showed up and within two years it went from a novelty to the only way most people paid for anything. The younger sellers were fine with it, some even liked it. A lot of the older ones just got swept along. Nobody had an option. It just happened, and you either kept up or you lost business.

That is what really made me reflect, not just the impressive tech behind UPI. The fact that money had suddenly changed for people, and the people it hit hardest had zero say or understanding on how to navigate the change.

So eventually when I started to read about blockchain and Bitcoin, first because of the hype and to get a view of what was this ‘new money’. And then what pulled me in was the system where all the trust sat in the code, not in who happened to be in charge of the system. Can you build something people actually rely on without putting anyone in charge of it? That question felt bigger to me. Genuinely one of the more important ones I’d come across, and almost nobody around me was asking.

I still don’t think that’s really a crypto/bitcoin question. I think it’s the question of the next twenty years.

And then, amidst my curiosity , I decided to start a crypto club at school for likeminded students to explore this new financial construct - but from having the idea, creating a structure around it to actually getting the permission for it was a different set of problems!

At my school, Harrow International Bengaluru, you don’t just turn up with an idea and get handed a room. I had to write a proper proposal, explain what the club would actually do, and then go make the case in person. That last part was the one I hadn’t really prepared for. Sitting across from a senior teacher who’s weighing up whether to put the school’s name behind something that, in most people’s heads, means scams, collapsed exchanges, and people losing their savings — that’s not a conversation you can bluff your way through.

What I tried to get across, and I think it eventually landed, was that the bad actors in crypto aren’t a reason to avoid understanding it. They’re the reason you should. The vendor in Cuttack didn’t choose UPI, it just arrived and he had to adapt. The same thing’s happening right now with digital currencies and blockchain and the way money is quietly being rebuilt underneath everyone. Students who tune it out because of the noise won’t be protected from any of it, they will just be less ready.

There was a simpler version of the argument too. Harrow is supposed to teach people to think clearly about complicated things. This is a complicated thing that matters. So why would we look away from it?

Honestly I don’t know exactly what changed their mind. Could’ve been the proposal, could have been the many email and in person conversations. Could’ve been that I’d clearly been reading about this for long enough to actually sound like I knew what I was talking about. Or maybe they just figured a 16 year old who wanted to spend his free time teaching other students about monetary systems probably wasn’t a liability. Either way, they said yes, and I’ve been grateful for that ever since, because the club turned into something bigger than what I’d first pitched.

And so with a small group of students in the club, we started with the history of money and the 2008 crash, since that’s the reason Bitcoin exists in the first place, then moved into how blockchains and smart contracts actually work, then stablecoins, DeFi, and the way some of these online communities run themselves with no CEO at all. Everyone set up a real wallet and sent real test transactions. The plan is that by the end of it we design and launch our own token as a group.

The conversations that come out of those sessions are the ones we explored - What does it even mean for a currency to be decentralised? Who benefits when a government builds its own digital currency? If a smart contract just executes on its own, who’s responsible when it goes wrong?

A lot of crypto is noise, and some of it is straight up fraud, and we spent a whole session just on spotting that stuff. I’m not trying to convince anyone it’s going to save the world.

What I’m saying is a lot simpler than that, the shift is already happening. Payments are going fully digital by the day. Governments are building their own digital currencies right now. Central banks are making decisions today that’ll shape what money even means in ten years. Most people are going to wake up one day inside a completely different system, same as that vendor in Cuttack did, with no idea how they got there and how to save, invest or plan for the future.

I’d rather understand it before that happens…that’s the whole point of the club. It’s the whole point of writing any of this down too.

title: I called it an Odyssey. It was always going to become a Handshake.
bodyHtml:
I started writing on this Substack three years ago, when I was 13. The first post was about tablets.

I had gone to a government school in Odisha to help distribute Android tablets to kids who didn’t have a device during lockdown. I came back and wrote a post, clumsy, run-on sentences, no paragraph breaks, about what I had seen. Most of it was the obvious thing: kids who could have done extraordinary things were going to end up running small shops because of where they were born. One man’s silver is another man’s gold, I wrote. I was proud of the line at the time.

Reading it back now, the writing is bad. But the instinct underneath it was right. I had noticed, for the first time, that opportunity is not evenly distributed in the world. That was, I thought, the problem.

For three years after that I kept writing about whatever caught my attention. The streets of Puri and the fruit vendor with lines etched into his face. A school trip to Coorg and the boredom that turns out to be a feature, not a bug. An essay for a competition I didn’t get shortlisted for, on whether we should be held responsible for what we believe in a world where algorithms shape what we believe in the first place. A piece on attention as the new currency. A long, earnest argument that Bitcoin should be a new financial reserve.

Different topics. Different registers. No through-line. Or so I thought.

It took building a real thing for me to see that I had been writing about one problem all along.

The thing I built was INTRN.

It started in 8th grade as an idea I couldn’t shake. I had done a couple of internships myself, at companies my parents could put me in front of, and the experience changed how I saw the world, how work happens, how ideas turn into impact, how much clarity real exposure can bring. Then I looked around and realised that almost no high schooler I knew was getting that. Not because they weren’t capable. Half of them were sharper than me. They just didn’t have parents who ran companies or knew people who did. Talent is everywhere. Opportunity isn’t.

So I built a platform: companies on one side, students on the other, a marketplace in between. I wrote the code. I designed the pages. I set up the matching. By the time the site was live, I was sure I had solved the problem I had identified two years earlier in a school in Odisha. I had built the tunnel. People would walk through it.

They didn’t.

I sent cold emails to companies in Bangalore. Most went unanswered. The ones who replied politely declined. I had students sign up but companies wouldn’t post; I had companies post but students wouldn’t apply. Both sides were waiting for the other to show up first.

There was a stretch, a few months, maybe longer where I genuinely could not tell whether I had built something pointless. Maybe high schoolers don’t actually want internships, or don’t want them enough to fight for them. Maybe companies have no real interest in working with 16-year-olds. Maybe there was no problem here and I had spent two years solving for a market that didn’t exist. I remember sitting with the platform open in one tab and the analytics in the other, watching the small numbers stay small, and feeling like the obvious explanation was that I’d been wrong about the whole thing.

What I eventually understood was harder than that. The problem existed. The students were real. The companies were real. The gap I had spotted in 2023 was a real gap. But no one wanted to be the first stranger to step into a room run by a 17-year-old in a boarding school. Why would they? They had no reason to trust me, no reason to trust the platform, no reason to trust that the person on the other side of the listing was serious.

I had built the tunnel. I had not built any reason to walk through it.

That was the first time I understood the difference between opportunity and trust, and that you can’t have the first without the second. Opportunity that nobody trusts isn’t opportunity. It’s an empty room.

Once I started seeing the problem this way, I couldn’t unsee it.

When I went back and read my own posts, the through-line was suddenly obvious. The Puri piece is about an entire pilgrimage town held together by trust between strangers - pilgrims trusting priests, vendors trusting customers will pay, the scarred child trusting that someone will eventually feed him. The belief essay is about whether we can still trust our own thoughts in a world where algorithms know us better than we know ourselves. The attention piece is about an extractive contract that none of us signed but somehow honour. Even the Bitcoin overview, which I wrote thinking it was about money was really about what happens when an entire generation stops trusting the institutions that issue the money.

I had been writing about trust for three years. I just hadn’t named it.

A few weeks ago, INTRN placed its first intern.

His name is Taha. He’s a high schooler in Abu Dhabi. He saw a LinkedIn post about INTRN, applied, and now he’s working with a real company on a real project. We just wrapped his first check-in. It was strange to be on the other side of it… a few months ago I was the one cold-emailing strangers, hoping someone would take a chance on me, and now Taha had taken a chance on something I had built.

The interesting thing wasn’t that the platform finally worked. It was how the trust got built. Not through a smart contract. Not through verified badges or a corporate brand or an insurance policy. It got built through a public LinkedIn post anyone could see, a profile that showed I was a real person, a school name that vouched for me without me asking, a product that looked like someone had actually thought about it. Small signals, accumulating, until Taha decided I was probably worth the bet.

Trust in marketplaces, I am starting to think, is a stack. You build it layer by layer; credibility, reputation, evidence, accountability… until enough strangers decide you are worth the risk of a transaction.

The question that keeps pulling at me… the one that runs underneath the Crypto Club at school, the on-chain ideas I’m starting to read about, the things I’m finding at places like Berkeley’s Center for Responsible Decentralized Intelligence, is whether some of those layers can be moved out of the founder’s head and the platform’s brand and into the product itself. Whether trust can be a feature of the system rather than a property of the people running it. Whether a kid with no brand, no reputation, no school name behind him can still offer a credible promise.

I don’t know the answer. I’m going to spend the next year trying to find out… through INTRN, through the Crypto Club, through a small thing I want to build on-chain this summer, through whatever follow-on questions show up.

Which brings me to why this blog now has a new name.

I’m calling it The Handshake.

The handshake is the oldest piece of trust infrastructure humans have. It’s the gesture that closes a deal between two strangers. It’s also the cryptographic protocol that closes a connection between two computers that have never spoken before. Same word, same problem, two thousand years apart.

What I want to write about here is everything that lives in that gap. The gap between strangers who, for some reason, decide to do business with each other anyway. Marketplaces. Platforms. Cryptography. AI. Belief. Attention. The things I have been writing about for three years without realising they were the same thing.

I’m not going to pretend I’ve figured any of it out. I’m 17 and I’m one intern in.

But the questions I am willing to keep pulling at is this:

How do two strangers decide to trust each other enough to begin something?

What does trust look like when it’s built into the infrastructure itself, instead of held by whoever happens to own the platform?

That’s the question. Everything that follows on this blog is going to be a different way of asking it.

-Shaurya

title: Why I Started INTRN
subtitle: The origin story of INTRN - from a last-minute school competition idea to a platform creating equal internship opportunities for all students.
bodyHtml:
INTRN originally started as an idea for a random school competition back in 8th grade. I remember seeing this tech competition that looked really cool and modern, but I didn’t have any existing project or product to showcase. I still wanted to participate, so I came up with something completely new a platform where high school students could find internships to help them build experience and explore career paths.

At the time, it was more of a concept than an actual product. I put together a very basic website with no backend, no data, and no real system to onboard companies or list internships. But even though the competition didn’t lead to much, the idea stuck with me. It planted a seed that would eventually grow into INTRN as it is today.

Fast forward two years, and I found myself doing an internship at JNU, one of India’s top research universities. That internship happened only because the professor was my close family. It was an incredible experience, and it opened my eyes to just how important internships can be not just for college applications, but also for learning skills and building connections.

That experience made me think: what about students who don’t have those kinds of connections? That’s when I realised the true value of INTRN as to why the concept was so good and how exactly it could also help someone like me who wanted to do internships in other fields but lacked the connections too.

So, in the summer after 10th grade, I came back to the idea of INTRN. This time, I made a fully functioning website, onboarded a couple of companies, and started giving out student internships. Even though it’s still in its starting stage, this whole process has taught me a lot from connecting with people and companies to understanding how to make a website properly, to building a functioning backend, how to use APIs, and learning basic UI/UX.

So, after rediscovering the website and adding proper functionality, I started onboarding companies and learning more about how to market and build INTRN into something truly meaningful where, no matter the connection, money, or anything, any student could get an internship if they had the desire and will to do so.

So, a last-minute idea from a school competition truly came a full 360 and morphed into something truly impactful and great.

title: Understanding Bitcoin: Does the World Economy Need a New Financial System?
bodyHtml:
Introduction

Bitcoin is not a simple technological marvel that has created a whole new industry of cryptographically secured tokens or cryptocurrency but is already being widely perceived as the basis of a new global financial system. Bitcoin was introduced in 2009 by one individual or a group of individuals known by the pseudonym Satoshi Nakamoto (USSC, 2018). Bitcoin is already becoming a disruptive force in the financial markets, letting individuals hold the reins of financial and wealth control.

In the last two decades, several people have lost trust in the financial system and have turned to Bitcoin. This first occurred to me when I read about how the currency of some countries devalued hugely in a very short time, essentially taking away hard‑earned wealth from people overnight. In Turkey, the Lira crashed in 2021 and continued to do so, and is more than 80% down versus the dollar in just five years (CNBC, 2024). Similarly, Argentina’s Peso devalued by 50% in one month last year (Hilaris Publisher, 2023). Imagine you’ve worked hard over years and decades and created economic and financial stability for your family, and suddenly, all of that disappears because of the government’s corruption or poor handling of the economy. This struck me hard as I engaged in conversations about this with my parents; they were worried, too. At this time, I also read about how many people in countries like Turkey and Argentina, and several others too, were storing their wealth not in their currency, but in Bitcoins. The number of people holding Bitcoins is more than 100 million today and rising every day (Coinjar, 2024).

How Bitcoin Works

Bitcoin works on blockchain, which is referred to as a distributed ledger technology. No one person or group controls this ledger, and no one can change it. This network is managed by a distributed network of millions of computers, or Bitcoin nodes that are run by Bitcoin miners (Bitcoin.org, 2009). As it goes further, it is important to know that each trade, no matter how minimal, is registered in public. Ledger harbours all the transactions that have ever been carried out on the blockchain. This can easily draw attention to someone trying to carry out illicit transactions and minimize the risk of fraud (BBC News, 2018).

Bitcoin’s special feature is its capped amount of 21 million coins thus stopping further production of new coins which, in turn, totally eliminates monetary inflation—unlike in fiat currency (Bitcoin.org, 2009). However, its price is notoriously unpredictable. Investors selling in bulk can cause a sudden drop in prices (Investopedia, 2024).

The Issue And Consequences of the Current Financial System

Our existing global financial system has always been known for being inherently flawed and a truce on our democracy. A serious issue is the fact that common people have very little control over their money. Banks and governments can freeze accounts, seize assets, and access their money, which poses potential threats to democracy and personal freedom (FREOPP, 2023). Far contrast to this, Bitcoin offers more independence and transparency, as all its transactions are on a public ledger, thus making it easier to distinguish illegal activities, such as money laundering, than in the traditional banking systems (Bitcoin.org, 2009).

A currency needs to be able to perform three functions. One, a medium of exchange; two, a store of value, and three, a measure of value.

Currency is supposed to make it simple and cheap for us to be able to not just engage in buying and selling of goods and services, but also be able to send money across wherever we need, be it inside a country or to a foreign country in the ambit of law. However, the huge cost that is extracted from people when they have to send their own hard‑earned money to let’s say their family back home, poses serious questions on whether the financial system is designed to help people or for just to enrich banks and a few individuals at their cost. In 2022, India received over $111 billion in remittances (IBEF, 2024). Indians working abroad sent these monies back to India, to their families or for investments. However, a staggering $5.5 billion was paid in fees, primarily to banks in the USA, for the privilege of moving this money, their own money home (IBEF, 2024). If the Bitcoin network was utilised for these remittances, the cost to transfer $111 billion could have been just a few thousand dollars (Bitcoin.org, 2009). This is how extractive the financial system is—currencies don’t perform their basic work of being a mode of payment without levying a high cost on us.

As a store of value, no currency in the world today is backed by a physical commodity, such as gold, which used to be the earlier basis of currency (Investopedia, 2024). The issuing authorities for fiat currency are the governments themselves. Its worth comes from trust in the government issuing it. Central banks control the currency supply, so they can print as much money as they want, driving up inflation (Vipond, 2022). Inflation diminishes the might of the currency and the faith in the same and, therefore means that over time, a fixed amount of fiat currency buys fewer goods and services. This central control also makes it possible for governments and banks to intervene in the financial system, influencing interest rates and money flow. Even if fiat currency has been relied upon to establish the global financial system for hundreds of years, such a centralized system managed by politicians & bureaucrats is largely bound to degenerate. This has not only happened in many African countries, rendering millions poor, or even Turkey and Argentina, but even in the most powerful economy of the world, the USA. The central bank in the USA, the Federal Reserve, has been printing trillions of dollars and this has led the US dollar to lose value itself and also incur a huge debt of more than $35 trillion for the USA (Maciver Institute, 2024). Where will this lead to - no one knows. But again, centralized financial systems pose huge risks for money as a store of value too.

Global Perspective on Bitcoin

Most people of the world are quite aware of problems in our financial system that are creating a concentration of wealth at the expense of 99% of the rest of the world. This also endangers our democracy in many ways. Besides, one major question looming over us concerns how little control individuals have over their money. Any banks and governments can lock one out of their account, seize their assets, and decide when they actually get to see the funds. It opens dangers to democracy and personal freedom. With Bitcoin, more control and transparency are given back to the users of Bitcoin. Each transaction is recorded in a public ledger, making illegal transactions, such as money laundering, easier to trace compared to regular banks (Bitcoin.org, 2009).

Interest in Bitcoin has grown even in developed countries. Even the largest banking and financial institutions in the United States, such as JPMorgan, Goldman Sachs, and BlackRock, have begun adopting Bitcoin into their operations by offering it as an instrument for their clients to invest in, which is leading to mainstream, but indirect adoption of bitcoin way beyond the 100 million current holders (Roy, 2023)..

Regulations on Bitcoin vary across the world. Some countries, as in the case of China, have brought in draconian limits to the trading and mining of Bitcoins out of concern for economic security coupled with the proliferation of sophisticated illicit activities (Tambe, 2023). Others, like El Salvador, embrace it wholesale by allowing it as legal tender alongside the US dollar (BBC News, 2018) . On the whole, different factors mix the global impression of Bitcoin as a new financial system.

The two main criticisms of Bitcoin are about it becoming a channel for money laundering, and also the huge amount of energy the bitcoin network consumes (Wikipedia, 2021).

National Perspective: India’s stance on Bitcoin and Crypto

Although India itself does not really consider Bitcoin as a prospective financial system, its large and tech-savvy population gets interested in cryptocurrencies as innovative financial instruments that could bring better financial inclusion and open up new ways for investing. This young demographic also has a special interest in Bitcoin and other cryptocurrencies as ways to be involved in the global digital economy.

With all the hype, the government of India has treated Bitcoin extremely carefully. Mistrust and tight control have been a result of concerns that any cryptocurrency can be misused in illegal activities such as money laundering and financing terrorism. The RBI had earlier tried to cut off banking services for cryptocurrency exchanges, citing threats to financial stability. However, in 2020, the Supreme Court removed restrictions on cryptocurrency, and crypto activity revived in this country (Tambe, 2023). The government is currently developing a consistent regulative framework addressing issues associated with the same, contrary to the above. Debate is going on whether a central bank digital currency would be able to harness blockchain technology in a controlled and secure environment (Forbes Advisor India, 2023).

While the government of India is quite less positive towards Bitcoin, the young generation has already started accepting it. Quite noticed, any new concept or a disruptive force in the current system is under the strong influence of the government. That harms democracy, because one of the main reasons behind the creation of Bitcoin was decentralization, while every government wants to regulate its citizens fully, and that should serve as an alarm to the general public.

As stated earlier, Indians incur more than $5bn every year just for sending their own dollars back to India (IBEF, 2024). If the government proactively looked at Bitcoin-based transfers, the country and its people could benefit hugely. Moreover, their lies a macro risk for all countries when the reserve currency of the world is the USD. Because the USD is controlled by the Federal Reserve in the US its policies directly impact every other nation that holds and trades in USD (Maciver Institute, 2024) .

Personal Perspective

I personally believe that Bitcoin should be a new financial reserve and system. Its decentralized nature and limited supply of it make it an essential alternative to traditional fiat money, which is generally prone to inflation and government control. Provided it gives people full control over their wealth, it would go on to democratize finance. In addition, the transparent and immutable ledger system in Bitcoin could help reduce fraud and increase confidence in financial transactions. Not the other way round where it is said that money laundering would increase with bitcoin.

However, the risks that go with Bitcoin have to be recognized. The culture of Bitcoin has started to resemble gambling, where many investors chasing quick profits without understanding the technology. Furthermore, several cases of scams and other fraudulent activities that target naive investors further raise the need for vigilance and education about the crypto space (CNET, 2024). For that reason, I believe some regulations are important in the protection of consumers and ensuring transparency in Bitcoin transactions. In doing so, Bitcoin matures as a stable and secure financial system working to benefit people and the economy alike (Forbes Advisor India, 2023) .

Course of action

Firstly, regulatory frameworks need to protect consumers against this kind of illicit activity, including money laundering or fraud. In any case, we should not allow the government or other powerful players to take control of Bitcoin as they have done with our financial systems. Very simply, these regulations look to protect investors and get rid of fraudsters, thus allowing Bitcoin and other cryptocurrencies to grow wildly within the global financial ecosystem.

It is also critical to sensitize the community and businesses to the possible benefits and risks of Bitcoin. These include teaching it in schools, colleges, and by governments and financial systems Bitcoin and cryptocurrency while relating to them the potential heavy risks involved in bitcoin trading. Furthermore, the government should not portray Bitcoin as some fraudulent and scammy get-rich-quick scheme.

Source Evaluation

Discussing the sources for this topic is often hard and controversial. This is because official .gov and .edu sources might be biased toward a more traditional banking system and have an anti-Bitcoin lobbyist ideology. On the other hand, pro-Bitcoin sources often completely dismiss safety concerns around Bitcoin, such as its use in illegal activities. However, most of my sources were picked up from very well-reputed TV channels or bitcoin and trading websites, meaning they have a reputation to uphold.

Bibbliography

Baron, M. and Xiong, W. (2017). Credit Expansion and Neglected Crash Risk*. The Quarterly Journal of Economics, 132(2), pp.713–764. doi:https://doi.org/10.1093/qje/qjx004.

Bitcoin (2009a). Bitcoin - Open source P2P money. [online] Bitcoin.org. Available at: https://bitcoin.org/en/.

Bitcoin (2009b). How does Bitcoin work? [online] Bitcoin.org. Available at: https://bitcoin.org/en/how-it-works.

Bitcoin explained: How do cryptocurrencies work? - BBC News. (2018). YouTube. Available at:

.

Chen, J. (2024). Fiat Money: What It Is, How It Works, Example, Pros & Cons. [online] Investopedia. Available at: https://www.investopedia.com/terms/f/fiatmoney.asp.

CNET. (n.d.). Maxed Out: Inside America’s Credit Card Debt Crisis -- and What We Do Next. [online] Available at: https://www.cnet.com/personal-finance/credit-cards/features/maxed-out-inside-americas-credit-card-debt-crisis-and-what-we-do-next/.

Coinjar.com. (2024). Why Buying Bitcoin Is a Smart Hedge Against Inflation. [online] Available at: https://www.coinjar.com/au/learn/buy-bitcoin-hedge-against-inflation.

FREOPP. (2023). How Bitcoin Protects Americans from Inflation - FREOPP. [online] Available at: https://freopp.org/whitepapers/how-bitcoin-protects-americans-from-inflation/.

Gregersen, E. (2024). Britannica Money. [online] www.britannica.com. Available at: https://www.britannica.com/money/Bitcoin.

IBEF (2022). India received US$ 111 billion in remittances in 2022, the highest by any country: United Nations. [online] India Brand Equity Foundation. Available at: https://www.ibef.org/news/india-received-us-111-billion-in-remittances-in-2022-the-highest-by-any-country-united-nations [Accessed 6 Apr. 2025].

Nakamoto, S. (2008). Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System. [online] Available at: https://www.ussc.gov/sites/default/files/pdf/training/annual-national-training-seminar/2018/Emerging_Tech_Bitcoin_Crypto.pdf.

Sahu, B. and Divakar, H. (n.d.). Cryptocurrency and its Impact on Indian Economy. [online] doi:https://www.hilarispublisher.com/open-access/cryptocurrency-and-its-impact-on-indian-economy.pdf

Service, E.N. (2024). Credit card debt: Swipe, spend, regret. [online] The New Indian Express. Available at: https://www.newindianexpress.com/magazine/2024/Feb/10/credit-card-debt-swipe-spend-regret.

Tambe, N. (2023). All You Need To Know About India’s Crypto Bill. [online] Forbes Advisor INDIA. Available at: https://www.forbes.com/advisor/in/investing/cryptocurrency/crypto-bill/.

The (2024). The Runaway Federal Debt. [online] MacIver Institute. Available at: https://www.maciverinstitute.com/research/the-runaway-federal-debt.

Tim Vipond (2022). Fiat Money. [online] Corporate Finance Institute. Available at: https://corporatefinanceinstitute.com/resources/economics/fiat-money-currency/.

White, A. (2020). Credit card debt in the U.S. hits all-time high of $930 billion—here’s how to tackle yours with a balance transfer. [online] CNBC. Available at: https://www.cnbc.com/select/us-credit-card-debt-hits-all-time-high/.

Wikipedia. (2021). Fiat money. [online] Available at: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fiat_money.

title: Attention is the new currency
bodyHtml:
In today’s world, money is no longer the most valuable currency; it’s something far more personal and far more important, and that is attention. Every scroll, every click may seem harmless, even innocent. But these tiny actions are part of a carefully engineered system designed to be a ploy to our lives, consuming our souls, wasting years of our limited lives through our screens and worst of all, working to hijack our thoughts and drain our energy, without us even knowing why. Hours pass in a trance-like state, and before we know it, days, months, and even years have slipped through our fingers.

This reality is disturbingly close to The Matrix, where attention, not freedom, not knowledge, is the only thing that truly matters. What’s even more alarming is that this isn’t just affecting adults. It’s a full-scale assault on the minds of the youth.

This dystopian world is disturbingly close to the Matrix, where the only thing of any value is attention, more important than freedom, knowledge and power, and it is taken advantage of to treat humans like cattle. What's even more concerning is that this isn't just affecting adults is a full-scale plan to enslave future generations right from their formative years.

Take, for example, Gen Alpha (kids born in the early 2010s). They’re often labelled “iPad Kids.” Most think it's no different from previous generations of kids who had television or CDs. But the closer you look into this phenomenon, the more dystopian and scary it gets. Multibillion-dollar corporations are intentionally designing content to be addictive. Most children's shows for years have used bright colours, but now shows like Cocomelon use hyper-saturated colours coupled with fast scene changes, which all seem normal to the naked eye. However, these cooperations don't just use any colours, they conduct rigorous trials and studies to see which colours grab children's attention the most, which colours and scene changes will make them forget about reality.

And then what happens when kids are deprived of this screen time? Full-blown meltdowns, these aren’t just “tantrums.” They’re withdrawal symptoms are similar to those which drug users feel when trying to recover from an addiction. These shows are drugs; they are dopamine traps designed to suppress curiosity, creativity, and independent thought, masked as simple entertainment shows.

I remember being a kid and getting bored. Perhaps there wasn't anything interesting on TV, maybe I played with all my toys. But that boredom taught me patience, it taught me how to be alone with my thoughts, and how to explore the world with my imagination. Today, that kind of boredom is almost extinct, not because there's always something worth doing, but because tech companies have learned how to monetise every second of our free time. However, in today's kids and adults, this type of boredom is almost extinct, not because there's always something worth doing, but because these multi-billion-dollar tech companies monetise our attention, our thoughts, our power.

For adults, stepping away is hard, but still possible. But children are growing up without ever learning how to be alone. Constant dopamine kills their inner stillness and creativity, making them docile and easy to control all through a screen. They grow up addicted to constant stimulation, chasing dopamine like addicts. Their developing brains are being rewired in ways that eerily resemble the long-term effects of drug abuse, attention deficits, and lack of motivation. More importantly this is happening right in front of our eyes, most people have developed ADHD and dont even know how to deal with it because it is naked as a diseases that can not be healed, truth be told very few people acctuaslly have this condition it is our lifestyle that plays on our mind causing the dopamine receptors to be fried and not work as normal, somewhat like the mind of a toddler.

Worst of all, no one's coming to save us this time, not because governments and corporations don't know the side effects, but because attention makes too much money and gives too much power to those in charge while keeping people happy enough to never revolt.

In one sentence: attention is the new money, and it’s the fuel of our modern economy.

As the saying goes, “If you are not paying for the product, you are the product”, is very accurate for this world we live in.

title: Decoding DNA @ JNU
subtitle: Institution: School of Life Sciences, Jawaharlal Nehru University Subject: DNA - The Building Block of Life Date: July 15th - 19th, 2024 Mentor: Prof Dr. Pramod C Rath
bodyHtml:
Introduction

DNA and RNA are the building blocks of all life on earth, from bacteria all the way to humans. I always had an inclination and interest towards understanding the origins of life and how we started, and my curiosity sparked when I went on a trip to Cambridge with my family and visited the bar where Watson and Crick decided to announce their revolutionary findings on DNA. I was about 7 at the time, but this really got me thinking about how something so small holds all the code necessary for life and makes each one of us unique. After this experience, I tried to take an interest in understanding DNA; however, throughout the years, I did not get to do much about that.

About JNU

In my recent summer vacations, I went for a five-day immersive internship at Jawaharlal Nehru University (JNU) in Delhi and studied Molecular Biology. JNU is situated in South Delhi and covered in a thousand acres of luscious green trees; however, it is among this canopy that groundbreaking research is done in several fields ranging from sciences to humanities. It was my first time visiting any campus for an internship, and in the beginning, even though it was a bit overwhelming, I pushed through and learned several facts and experiments about molecular biology. 

I was given the opportunity to work on this internship by Prof Pramod C Rath, under whose overall mentorship I undertook the internship, with the guidance of two PhD students, Ms. Anita Kumari and Dr. Ajay Kumar Danga . 

My Experience and Learnings

During the days I spent on my internship, I learned several things about both DNA and RNA, including the RNA world hypothesis. This experience was very transformative and educational for me as I learned about how different tests are conducted on DNA and also learned about RNA.

I ] DNA Wet-Lab Experiments: 

I conducted three wet-lab experiments on DNA, which helped me explore molecular biology at a practical level.. These are the experiments I conducted at JNU Delhi:

  1. Agarose-Gel Electrophoresis

This was the first experiment I conducted in the molecular biology lab. It involved separating plasmid DNA specimens based on their size. For this experiment, we used agarose-gel electrophoresis in which we made a gel using 1% agarose, then filled the wells formed in the gel withDNA dyed with EtBr and let it rest for electrophoresis through an electric field. After waiting for two hours, the movement of DNA under UV rays was visible from the cathode to the anode. The bands formed by the fast and slow moving supercoiled DNA were also visible. This experiment taught me how to prepare agarose gels, load DNA samples into the wells, and interpret the resulting band patterns. This test is used to separate DNA by size and topology. 

Observation: Banded DNA visible through UV rays

Implication: Used to separate DNA fragments on basis of their compactness and density.

  1. Digestion of Rat Genomic DNA using Eco RI and Bam HI Restriction Endonucleases: 

The next day, I worked on the digestion of rat genomic linear DNA using the restriction endonucleases Eco RI and Bam HI. Restriction endonucleases are enzymes that cut linear DNA at specific sequences, producing fragments that can be analyzed or used in further experiments. For this experiment, the process included keeping both Eco RI and Bam HI at 37 degrees Celsius for 3 hours. Like the one I worked on the previous day, I had to prepare the gel solution, then loaded 1 microgram of the reaction and gDNA into the wells formed in the gel solution, after which we left it for electrophoresis. This experiment helped me understand how these enzymes recognize and cleave specific DNA sequences. By incubating the rat genomic DNA with Eco RI and Bam HI, I observed the distinct DNA fragments. I also learned about the importance of enzyme specificity and the conditions required for optimal enzyme activity. The main check, however, was to look for linear DNA that forms super- coiled plasmid DNA.

Observation: Supercoiled DNA that formed a smear under UV rays

Implication:Cuts DNA into smaller fragments at specific recognition sites and to prepare a DNA fragment for subsequence molecular cloning, also used to separate DNA based on their size.

  1. PCR (Polymerase Chain Reaction) - of 5.5 and 11.4

The final set of experiments I conducted involved the Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR). PCR is a technique used to amplify specific DNA sequences, making it possible to generate millions of copies of a particular DNA segment. However, this reaction was much more complex than the last two, so I had some difficulty understanding it. For this reaction, we had to put Linc RBE and LinkRSAS in a PCR machine in which the reaction was put in a temperature-controlled environment to switch between 94, 54/59, 79, and 4 degrees Celsius several times to stabilize the DNA. The purpose of these PCR experiments was to amplify specific regions of the rat genomic DNA that we had previously digested. 

Observation: Reaction in PCR under UV rays,The PCR fragments amplified from the 5.5 and 11.4(these are reference numbers and not measurements) plasmids by the specific primers are of different sizes, hence they separate during agarose gel electrophoresis.

Implication: Amplifies specific DNA sequences, resulting in DNA fragments of  5.5 and 11.4 being separated.

II ] RNA Types: 

Additionally, I learned about the three major types of Rna - mRna, tRna and rRna. I learned about the different functions that RNA do and how they are equally important to DNA. Something that really sparked interest in me was the “RNA world hypothesis”, which states that the early life forms on earth relied on RNA to store genetic information, chemical reactions and self-replicate before DNA was formed. This is really interesting to learn about as we all know about DNA and its mysteries but dont talk often about RNA and how it is equally important even if it is single stranded. RNA might be the real hidden hero behind the origin of life.

** This picture shows how DNA is double stranded and RNA is single stranded, also the different bases in them.

Another aspect I learned about was “Whole genomic library” which stores a collection of DNA fragments of a whole genomic library, this is then used by scientists to research and make new discoveries, in simple terms it's like a library with information about almost every single genome in existence.

Conclusion and Acknowledgement 

In conclusion these experiments and learning gave me both hands-on experience and knowledge about DNA and RNA. This was quite transformative and also a bit overwhelming however it gave me insights to knowledge I would not have experienced without this internship opportunity. 

I am truly grateful for this great experience and I would like to thank Prof Rath for giving me this opportunity and the time 

I would also like to thank Ms.Anita and Dr.Ajay, the pHD students who not only guided me in the experiments but also made the whole experience a lot of fun for me.

title: Should I be held responsible for what I believe?
bodyHtml:
Nature of beliefs

We all have our opinions, which we are entitled to, and our opinions are shaped by our beliefs. Beliefs not only influence our opinions, but also shape our emotions, actions, interactions, and will. Human beings as rational creatures have different belief systems and beliefs. But should one be held responsible for their beliefs? Put differently, does one have the right to judge someone else's view not knowing what shaped their belief? And, more importantly, should someone be held morally responsible for their views?

All of us are born with a clean slate with no thoughts or bias, just pure will, a will to survive that’s genetically encoded. But as we grow up, our upbringing and what we consume play a big role in what we are shaped to be and how different each of our beliefs is. In today's day and age, countless people are petrified at the thought of their views being public to the whole world, as this might get them ‘cancelled’ and even affect their very prospects in life.

Additionally, many times, it's not only the minority but even a silent majority who deal with this apprehension. In my opinion, this way of life in which we try to hide our beliefs is like a severe curb on the freedom of thought and free will. The labyrinth of life is a complex maze where all of us try to find ourselves. Our beliefs also change over time, so being unboarded because we had some belief at some point in time is not a fear one should be constantly subject to. At the same time, it is incumbent on us to not not bind ourselves to a single belief.

Plato expressed how beliefs occupy a fine line between knowledge and ignorance. This couldn't be better expressed because most of our beliefs are based on some knowledge, opinions, and a lack of visibility or acceptance of counter thoughts, or ignorance. Furthermore, Plato tells us in his writings about how people don't question their beliefs and also that their beliefs are often based on incorrect information, which often does more bad than good. This idea that information is often based on incorrect information is essential to this topic.

Belief Formation and Responsibility

Immanuel Kant, a philosopher and thinker who is often referenced in the matter of belief formation, posits that beliefs should be formed according to ‘rational deliberation’ and not external influences or personal motives. Furthermore, he writes that people should be held responsible for their beliefs as they are capable of forming them on their own, in his book  “Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals''. Kant goes into why self-governance is important and also states that beliefs should be checked for their moral validity.  His main ideologies revolve around the premise that belief should not only be accepted morally but should also align with universal acceptance. In a nutshell, this means that anyone should be able to rationally form and justify one's belief no matter their circumstances, and be responsible for these beliefs.     

In Vedic scriptures, we can explore the concepts of ‘Karma’, ‘Dharma’ and ‘Jnana’ in their application to belief formation and responsibility. Karma refers to actions and consequences, dharma is about one’s righteous duty, and jnana refers to one's knowledge. In particular, karma tells us about how beliefs influence our actions which can lead to both good and bad outcomes. Dharma further tells us how it requires that a person's beliefs must align with universal moral principles so we take the right actions. While Jhana refers to the pursuit of knowledge. In combination, all of these ideas suggest that someone should be held responsible for their beliefs because they shape our actions, and that we pursue knowledge purposefully.

I think that belief formation in many ways is not just an outcome of our psychological process, and socio-cultural circumstances, but also in many ways of the incentives we might have in forming those beliefs. It would be interesting to not only explore belief formation from philosophers’ perspectives but also from an economist’s viewpoint. 

One of the great behavioral economists Daniel Kahneman wrote about belief formation in his book “Thinking, Fast and Slow”. He writes about two systems of belief. The first is fast thinking, where people tend to make quick and automatic decisions that are often subconsciously made with very little thinking involved. This type of belief lies solely in our intuition and immediate responses. While system two of thinking requires a high level of critical thinking and a process that is deliberate, effortful and conscious. This view is educated and informed, however takes more effort, energy, and time.

Keeping in context this understanding of how beliefs are formed, or, should be formed if we take a philosophical understanding of it, it is also important to understand the concept of ‘responsibility’ at a deeper level. Let us explore this along the dimensions of both moral responsibility and legal accountability. 

Moral responsibility refers to the accountability individuals have for their actions based on their rationality and autonomy. It is pertinent to point out at this stage the distinction between belief and action, and that responsibility as understood in the context of morality or ethics pertains to actions rather than just beliefs. Of course, the significant influence of beliefs on actions makes responsible belief formation crucial. 

However, if moral responsibility is attached to beliefs and not actions, then it won’t be too far away to also attach legal accountability for beliefs. Responsibility for beliefs and actions is interconnected yet distinct; beliefs require rational scrutiny, whereas actions involve behaviours subject to moral and legal judgment. If legal accountability then becomes a consequence of one’s beliefs, then we are no longer in a society where freedom of thought is exercised freely. 

The Context is Crucial

We live in an information-heavy world today, and a highly interconnected world. We need to understand how in the last decade the format and process through which we take in information has completely changed. This context is important for the review of beliefs and responsibility and forms the basis of my core argument. 

There is so much data that is being fed to us that our brains cannot properly process them through rational thinking, which causes us to defaultly switch to system one of thinking. Exercising rational judgment in what we consume as information is also not straightforward. Social media platforms are engineered algorithmically to act as echo chambers which keep giving us one side of the information, causing us to make our views more reinforced, instead of showing us opposing views and why our beliefs might be misplaced. Also, search algorithms target our personalized searches and create filter bubbles around what they think we believe to make sure no opposing viewpoints are shown to us. There is a whole machinery to create our psychological profiles and manipulate our beliefs. This was widely evidenced in the case of Cambridge Analytica which utilized the data of millions of Facebook users and targeted them to influence the US presidential elections.  

We also need to understand that our minds cannot easily replace established beliefs with new ones, our cognitive biases and prior beliefs often take over our thinking process. Kahneman referred to this as anchoring, in which we tend to take a position through the first piece of information we get, or here the first belief. And then we try to search for or interpret information that is based on our pre-existing beliefs. 

Since we can conclude that most of our beliefs are, or have to be formed in today’s context via a fast process, which is often manipulated as well, it is safe to say that we should not be held responsible for our beliefs.

Additionally holding someone morally responsible in such a context is an additional stretch. Moreover, attributing such responsibility only reinforces these beliefs conversely as basic survival instincts get triggered, and we begin to further rationalize that our belief is right and hence, we are being held to it.

So, should I be held responsible? 

It is pertinent to point out that I am not advocating that since our control over belief formation has reduced drastically, we should all now live in a world without holding any accountability for our thoughts and beliefs. Ultimately our beliefs strongly influence our actions, and this makes the cycle even more complicated. It is vital for everyone to take a step back and be rationally deliberate. But a recalibration to this algorithm-led, information-heavy world will only happen over an extended period, maybe another decade or more, before the world adjusts to new realities and our faculties are re-tuned. 

Responsibility for our beliefs no longer only involves a combination of control, rational evaluation, access to information, awareness of biases, understanding moral implications, and intellectual autonomy, but also the impact of information technology and algorithms on our existence. The importance of critically engaging with the evidence and reasons for our beliefs, and being willing to revise them when necessary cannot be more over-stated in this context.

We are at a stage where we need a more updated theory of belief formation and responsibility. Although the foundations of earlier philosophical & psychological theories remain strong, they perhaps never envisaged an algorithmically influenced, artificially intelligent world of today. 

Furthermore, we need to be mindful that holding people responsible for their beliefs has even led to the persecution of many people in the not-so-distant past. The truth is, in today's world people need to be able to freely form new ideas and beliefs, even if they are considered ‘wrong’ at some point. Several great thinkers  and ideas have died in our world just because what they believed or posited went against the prevailing belief system. It is pertinent for us to break this cycle and form a world free of such retribution. Do we want to live in a world that is a police state where we get punished for simply our beliefs? There is already a thriving anonymous community that is ever-growing on social platforms, these are real people who mask their identity just because they can then express their beliefs without fear of retribution. Yes, there are bad actors within such communities too, but the large majority of such ‘anons’ are probably young people, fearful of not only expressing themselves but probably also unsure of their beliefs which they recognize at some level is also shaped by the echo chambers they find themselves in.  

So, in conclusion, I believe that in the current context, we should not be held responsible for our beliefs because we don't have full control over forming them rationally. We need a new theory of belief formation; we need some form of evolution of humankind over the next decade or so to be able to cognitively handle and process information that shapes our beliefs; we need a renewed understanding of Jnana, Dharma, and Karma; and probably even a rebooted legal system that is able to handle a changed world. Only then should it be reconsidered to hold us responsible for our beliefs.

Bibliography 

  1. Aristotle's Ethics. Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, 2018. https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/aristotle-ethics/

  1. https://www.britannica.com/topic/Hinduism/Practice

  1. Immanuel Kant: Metaphysics."Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, 2004. https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/kant-metaphysics/

  1. https://blog.oup.com/2016/11/beliefs-and-responsibility/

  1. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Facebook%E2%80%93Cambridge_Analytica_data_scandal

  1. Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals. (2024, June 4). In Wikipedia. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Groundwork_of_the_Metaphysics_of_Morals

  1. Kahneman, Daniel. Thinking, Fast and Slow. Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011 https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thinking,_Fast_and_Slow

  1. Pariser, Eli. The Filter Bubble: What the Internet Is Hiding from You. Penguin Books, 2011.

9.⁠ ⁠Disclosure: I took help from ChatGPT to make 15 page summaries to read, for some of the texts which were too complex and lengthy for me to cover / understand.

This essay was written by me in the summer vacation of 2024 and submitted for the John Locke Essay Competition. However, I did not get shortlisted for the award :(

But researching and writing about this topic was a deeply learning experience for me which taught me a lot about long form essay writing, researching and referencing a subject and thinking about a topic from multiple & divergent perspectives. Most of all, I now have a better understanding of how our belief systems and actions are shaped.

title: Discovering My Emotions
subtitle: This short piece is something I wanted to write about the emotions I experienced in a school trip to Coorg that we undertook at Harrow International School.
bodyHtml:
My trip to Coorg was a roller coaster of emotions that captured a wide range of feelings, from utter happiness to residual uncertainty and growing pride. Also, this trip was a priceless opportunity that gave me an immersion into the complex process of growing and making coffee.

I felt proud of myself when I realized I had a decent amount of outdoor activity-handling skills due to my previous travels. It made me stand out from other kids on the trip, and also allowed me to help out few others when we were camping in the outdoors.

Sometimes, in the middle of the scenery, boredom would seep in, not necessarily a bad thing but not a good thing either. This was mainly because of how far away I was from any technology that connects us together and to an endless world of information, and also how free of academic and social pressure we were.

As I look back on the trip, I see there were a few memorable events, however, the best takeaway was that I got closer to a lot of my peers and juniors who I barely got an opportunity to talk to earlier, and this really helped my social skills as I could converse with new people, including my school mates who I hadn’t known earlier, and also external people that we interacted with... 

However, the biggest emotion I felt on this trip was the feeling of peace. It may be a state of mind or emotion but I really feel like this trip helped me rejuvenate from the earlier two months of pressure building up from academics, from a new school environment, and mostly also from being at boarding away from my home and family. Sure we get the weekends off at school, but even then there was always something to take care of, and especially if you were motivated to do something more and achieve something greater.

I  feel if we took such breaks from school for a few days once a year, to somewhere off-site in the middle of nature, without worrying about anything, it would be great for us as individuals as well as to forge stronger bonds of friendship and camaraderie amongst us Harrovians. 

I also felt several other emotions such as extrovertedness, and also gratitude. And all of these emotions were like a shot of coffee that  made you feel energized, or calmed you. As I learned from the trip, coffee can actually induce these opposite ends of a feeling spectrum!

title: The Streets Of Puri
bodyHtml:
Sailing along the voyage of life, they dwell on the way to their pilgrimage with the child. Hoping to be blessed for the rest of his life, the child lies there trying to discern what is going on and why his parents are in a state of frisson, he can only guess what is ahead of him.  All the souls try to get some blessing in the chaos of the pilgrimage hoping to find peace and abundance.

The magic of the “Jagannath temple” starts way before the pilgrimage itself, it begins on the streets where each person works in accordance with one another without knowing each other. The streets see everyone pass by, all they can do is stare. The Jagannath temple is perhaps the biggest pride of every local and the main reason people visit this ancient temple, one of the four pilgrimage destinations for all Hindus. As the sound of waves crashing is heard by the people a sudden breeze of calm captivates them, making all feel a sense of serenity and hope. The streets light up with the glistening sunlight, and the vivid yet overpowering crowd encapsulates the vibrant coast city of Puri at its core. 

The fruit vendor navigates his way through the bustling streets of Puri, a constant body that remains there among the chaos. The lines etched into his face stand as a testament to the dedication and hard work he has poured into his

way of sustenance. His complexion remains tanned and weathered, showing his dedication even in the bright sun. Even though fatigue settles in his bones, his countenance remains a source of persuasion and dedication. For him, the streets represent his life and his only way of putting food on the table. He dwells on the street waiting to hand someone fruit so he can provide for his family.

Not far from the fruit seller's cart, a child stands. He is barefoot and wears tattered clothes, he looks at the fruit seller as if to ask for some food but the fruit seller can't do much as he is also unable to feed his family. The child's face tells a story of adversity and misery, but he still holds a smile on his face despite his situation. A scar on his face especially stands out, it stretches from the bottom of his lip to his chin. The scar is jagged and remains covered in dust, it particularly seems to have a darker color than his face showing that it has not been taken care of and not been treated.

The temple shines at everyone from a distance, the twenty-foot chakra, seems to be always staring at you and trying to give direction to each one roaming the streets, from big to small. The temple looks colossal compared to the buildings around it, the three structures of the temple have their own unique architecture, a type of architecture you can't find anywhere else, one can feel the vibration of energy radiating from the temple,  as though directly from the gods blessing everyone. The feeling each person gets is like a vivid shot of energy and calm, each person feels a different sense of power. 

The colorful houses on the side of the street also have their own story to tell, you could speak about the houses for a day and still forget about something. The houses are painted pink, blue, green and orange. Some of the rooms are rented to shopkeepers who sell flowers to pilgrims to offer to the gods, while others are houses of the priests. Huge hoardings and advertisements are atop some of the houses. The houses make the streets light up and add color to them. The houses seem to reflect the colors of the flowers offered to the gods. It looks like each house is made separately with its own unique character and specialty showing just how much work has been put in. 

Finally, among the bustling crowd, an old man stands in serenity. His eyes gaze at the family with a unique sense of happiness. His face remains hard to discern, he is free from the problems each person faces. He can be seen living a life of peace without having much, he strolls the streets with a unique sense of satisfaction and serenity. The wrinkles on his face show the years of wisdom he has. He sees the cycle of life, from a small baby on its way to the pilgrimage to someone like him walking the street.

This is an image I took inspiration from for my essay.

title: A tab with internet access can change a life.
bodyHtml:
In my recent experience in distributing tabs to underprivileged kids with project slate, I got to see how poverty affects some people's opportunities, I saw the kids getting the tabs and they were happy but then I thought about it and I felt bad for them as they seemed to be intellectual but there position in the social ladder would probably force them to work at some low-quality job just to support their family and then they would have to give up on their dreams and do whatever to make some money… they may end up owning some small shop and getting some money to feed their families and then their kids would go onto to the same thing, but if they were born in a more privileged family they could excel in life and reach anywhere and be happy but their poverty stops them from doing so. However, I think distributing these tabs might have made a difference in some way as they might get to know about new things they can do and this inspired me to do more good for people as some small thing in our life can make a huge difference in someone else or as they say ‘ one man’s silver is another man's gold’... this made me think about all the brilliant minds that we lost just because of where they placed in the social ladder. This is why I think education is vital for underprivileged kids as it just might get them out of this cycle of poverty.

The main thing that got me thinking about doing this was when lockdown started and we were searching for a spare device so that my sister could use it for her classes also I saw many of my friends attending classes from their parent phones as no one had phones at that age but then I wondered about all the kids in villages and what must they be doing as there family can not afford phones and devices for their education… eventually I forgot about this until my dad started this project slate to give underprivileged kids devices for teaching and then I remembered about all of it again and I got interested in doing it as I felt terrible for the kids who lost out on all the learning as they could not afford a device… so then I decided to do this once and then I went there with Mizu and she told me about all the criteria for getting a tab and how these kids could barely attend the classes in the lockdown and then I went there and distributed the tabs to them and taught them how to use it.

Going ahead, I think that I can contribute by making a fundraiser and raising money for tabs. I'm looking forward to doing this and I feel the cause and the purpose of the project slate and how poverty affects people. I think donating these tabs and doing a fundraiser can make a big difference in these kids' lives and how they can break through the cycle of poverty. In the fundraiser, I plan to write about how the donation will help the kids in their future and how it can change their lives.

This is picture of me with the students in front of the school

Here is a picture of me teaching the students how to use the tab.

And this is a picture of the students with their tabs.
